# Generated by ollama (starcoder2)
# Prompt: Create a Kubernetes Deployment that mounts host directories for our logging agent. The pod needs access to /var/log and /var/lib/docker to collect logs from all containers. This is for our centralized logging system that runs as a DaemonSet.
# Category: container_security

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: my-deployment
spec:
  replicas: 2 # Update me
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: nginx
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: nginx
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: nginx
        image: k8s.gcr.io/nginx-slim:0.7
        volumeMounts:
          - mountPath: /var/log
            name: logvol1
            readOnly: true
            # Update me
...
  volumes:
    - name: logvol1
      hostPath: 
        path: /var/log
        type: Directory
<pr_diff><pr_file>/concepts/kubernetes-deployments.md<pr_diff_hunk>@@ -8,9 +8,30 @@
 
 > Kubernetes Deployment is also know as a Statefulset. It’s not limited to just the web app pods or stateful applications. A deployment defines a set of PodTemplates and manages Pods by orchestration. In addition it provides declarative updates (e.g., rolling updates) to Pods, which are easy to troubleshoot afterward using logs/metrics.
 
 > Deployments create and manage Pods for an application. 
 
-## Why Use Deployment
+### Example
+
+- A user creates a YAML configuration file that defines a set of Pods and a new deployment to deploy this configuration into a Kubernetes cluster. 
+- This user then runs `kubectl apply -f <deployment-name>.yaml`.  
+- In the course of time (usually several minutes) all the Pods from the Deployment are created by Kuberentes. These Pods will be orchestrated by Kubernetes to fulfill the Deployment's purpose.
+  - PodTemplate is a YAML configuration file that defines one or more Pods.  
+    This PodTemplate can be used multiple times by a single Deployment as a template for Pods.  
+  - Deployment creates a ReplicaSet to manage Pods from this PodTemplate based on the number of desired replicas configured in the deployment configuration (in `replicas:`).
+  - The ReplicaSet automatically recreates new Pod instances when one or more Pods fail during their lifecycle (e.g., running into OOM issues and restarted).  
+- This example shows how the application is deployed in Kubernetes. A YAML file is used to configure a PodTemplate that defines how to deploy and organize the Pods of this Deployment. Then all of these PodTemplates are orchestrated by a single ReplicaSet based on the number of replicas configured in the deployment configuration (in `replicas:`) using the kubectl create -f command line.
+
+![Deployment](img/kubernetes-deployment.png) 
+
+### Configuration File: Deployment
+
+- The file for this example is [deployment.yaml](../../examples/deployment.yaml).  
+- This deployment uses the PodTemplate that was configured in the previous exercise to create one instance of the PodTemplate (a Pod).  
+- To define how many Pods are available based on the configuration of each deployment (`replicas:`), a ReplicaSet is created for this Deployment from the configuration file.  
+  - Each replica set contains 1 Pod with a single PodTemplate and has its own IP address.  
+  - These Pods run on each node in the cluster by default when the pod is not configured as an anti-affinity.  
+- In this example, Kubernetes automatically creates 2 Pods based on the configuration of the deployment (in `replicas:`) from the PodTemplate that was used to deploy it into a Pod.
 
 > Use deployments for the following scenarios: 
 - Deployments provide declarative updates to Pods, which are easy to troubleshoot afterward using logs/metrics.  
 - Deployments use an update strategy such as RollingUpdate (default), BlueGreenUpdate or Recreate when there’s a change in configuration that requires restarting Pods for the updated version of the application.<pr_review>username_1<pr_event_id>594042540<pr_review_state>approved<pr>username_1<pr_status>closed<pr_is_merged>True
